title: Lock down your server with a firewall

series.requires_description: nginx must be proxying requests to Node app on port 3000

problem: Your app is running and accessible but your server is wide open. Any port that has a service on it is reachable from the internet. Lock it down — only SSH and HTTP should be accessible from outside. Everything else gets dropped.

setup.description: Check what is currently exposed on your server.
setup.note: ss shows every port currently listening. firewall-cmd checks if firewalld is already active on your Amazon Linux instance. Amazon Linux 2023 uses firewalld not ufw — if firewalld is running use firewall-cmd, if not we will set it up.

hints: Amazon Linux uses firewalld not ufw — the commands are firewall-cmd not ufw. Start with sudo systemctl start firewalld and sudo systemctl enable firewalld
Add rules for SSH and HTTP before enabling the firewall — if you block SSH before allowing it you will lock yourself out of the server permanently
firewall-cmd --permanent makes rules survive reboots, without --permanent rules are lost on restart. Always follow with firewall-cmd --reload to apply
solution:
1. Check listening ports:     sudo ss -tlnp  # see everything currently exposed
2. Install firewalld:         sudo yum install -y firewalld  # if not already installed
3. Start firewalld:           sudo systemctl start firewalld  # start the service
4. Enable on boot:            sudo systemctl enable firewalld  # survive reboots
5. Check current rules:       sudo firewall-cmd --list-all  # see default zone rules
6. Allow SSH first:           sudo firewall-cmd --permanent --add-service=ssh  # never lock yourself out
7. Allow HTTP:                sudo firewall-cmd --permanent --add-service=http  # port 80 for nginx
8. Reload rules:              sudo firewall-cmd --reload  # apply permanent rules
9. Verify rules:              sudo firewall-cmd --list-all  # confirm ssh and http are listed
10. Test SSH still works:     open a second terminal and SSH in  # confirm before closing current session
11. Test app still works:     curl http://YOUR_EC2_IP  # nginx should still respond
12. Test port 3000 blocked:   curl http://YOUR_EC2_IP:3000  # should timeout or refuse
